title: Machine learning driven inverse design of devices and components for optical communication and sensing systems: a comprehensive review
abstract: We discuss recent progress in using machine-learning (ML)-enabled inverse design techniques applied to photonic devices and components. Specifically, we highlight the design of optical sources, including fiber and semiconductor lasers, as well as Raman and semiconductor optical amplifiers. Although inverse design approaches for optical detectors remain relatively underexplored, we examine optical layers, particularly metamaterial absorbers, as promising candidates for high-performance optical detection. In addition, we underscore advancements in inverse designing passive optical components, including beam splitters, gratings, and optical fibers. These optical blocks are fundamental in developing next-generation standalone optical communication systems and optical sensing networks, including integrated sensing and communication technologies. While categorizing various reported deep learning architectures across five paradigms, we offer a paradigm-based perspective that reveals how different ML techniques function within modern inverse design methods and enable fast, data-driven solutions that significantly reduce design time and computational demands compared with traditional optimization methods.

Abstract. We discuss recent progress in using machine-learning (ML)-enabled inverse design techniques 
applied to photonic devices and components. Specifically, we highlight the design of optical sources, 
including fiber and semiconductor lasers, as well as Raman and semiconductor optical amplifiers. Although 
inverse design approaches for optical detectors remain relatively underexplored, we examine optical layers, 
particularly metamaterial absorbers, as promising candidates for high-performance optical detection. In 
addition, we underscore advancements in inverse designing passive optical components, including beam 
splitters, gratings, and optical fibers. These optical blocks are fundamental in developing next-generation 
standalone optical communication systems and optical sensing networks, including integrated sensing and 
communication technologies. While categorizing various reported deep learning architectures across five 
paradigms, we offer a paradigm-based perspective that reveals how different ML techniques function 
within modern inverse design methods and enable fast, data-driven solutions that significantly reduce 
design time and computational demands compared with traditional optimization methods.
Keywords: machine learning; deep learning; inverse design; photonic device; semiconductor laser; fiber laser; Raman amplifier; 
semiconductor optical amplifier; fiber amplifier; optical fiber; power splitter; grating; fiber Bragg grating; metagrating; coupler; 
metamaterial absorber.

1 Introduction
The rapid advancement in communication infrastructure de-
mands more complex integration of diverse systems, where vari-
ous networks are interconnected to create scalable architectures. 
As we move toward ultrafast speeds and low-latency networks, 
the application paradigm is rapidly evolving, from Internet-of-
Things-based communication devices to advanced sensing 
applications. 1 In this context, optical communication and sens-
ing technologies are essential for achieving the goals of such 
modernized infrastructures. 2 These optical technologies are ex-
pected to support both guided and unguided systems by integrat-
ing advanced fiber-optic designs with optical wireless networks, 
such as those operating in free space and underwater environ-
ments. This will enable ultra-high-speed optical communica-
tions for emerging network paradigms, including seamless 
connectivity across air, water, and space as part of the Internet

of Everything (IoE). 3 In addition to communication, they are 
increasingly used for optical sensing in integrated sensing 
and communication (ISAC) systems. Furthermore, standalone 
optical sensing solutions are also expected to play a significant 
role in the evolution of the IoE. As a result, developing optical 
technologies for communication, dedicated sensing, and ISAC 
systems will be crucial for meeting the increasing demands of 
multidisciplinary applications in areas such as environmental 
monitoring, healthcare, industrial automation, intelligent trans-
portation, and the marine environment.
Optical devices and photonic components form the founda-
tion of photonic technologies, as the performance and efficiency 
of many photonic systems are primarily defined by their optical 
elements. 4 For example, in an optical communication system, 
key performance metrics such as data throughput, bandwidth 
(BW) scalability, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and bit error rate 
mainly rely on device and component optimization. Similarly, a 
targeted optical sensing application may require high sensitivity, 
a wide dynamic range, and precise spectral resolution. An ISAC 
system might need to optimize parameters for both optical com-
munication and sensing functions at the same time. As optical 
systems grow more complex and larger, careful design of their 
photonic components becomes essential to meet strict applica-
tion needs.
A wide variety of optical building blocks essentially deter-
mines the system’s functionality, which includes optical sources 
(e.g., semiconductor lasers, fiber lasers, and light-emitting 
diodes), passive components (e.g., power splitters, wavelength-
selective filters, and mode couplers), optical amplifiers, optical 
fibers, and photodetectors. Each of these plays a key role in 
achieving the desired performance of the system for a dedicated 
application. For instance, designing broadband optical ampli-
fiers covering all five telecommunication bands (i.e., O, E, S, 
C, and L) is crucial for ultra-wideband (UWB) networks. 
Raman amplifiers (RAs), with their low noise and multiband 
gain, are well suited for this purpose, though their design re-
mains challenging due to complex dependencies on fiber prop-
erties, pump configurations, and nonlinear effects. 5 On the other 
hand, optical sources with application-specific characteristics 
are essential as sensing systems often require broadband light 
sources for accurate detection, whereas optical communication 
relies on narrowband lasers for high-speed data transmission, 
and multiwavelength lasers are key to enabling wavelength-
division multiplexed (WDM) systems.
Hence, in the design of large-scale photonic systems, a top-
down approach is generally considered by decomposing the sys-
tem into modular subsystems (e.g., transmitter and receiver), 
each with clearly defined performance objectives. Next, the op-
tical components within these subsystems must be individually 
engineered to meet local targets, which cumulatively contribute 
to achieving the overall performance goals of the subsystems 
and hence the optical system. This modular approach enables 
systematic optimization, improves design scalability, and facil-
itates the development of highly efficient, application-specific 
optical technologies. A typical taxonomy illustration, mapping 
various application areas to optical element classes, is shown 
in Fig. 1.
In recent years, inverse design (ID) has emerged as a trans-
formative approach in developing targeted-performance optical 
devices and components. 6–9 By allowing designers to specify 
desired optical functionalities and automatically find the corre-
sponding physical structures, inverse design has revolutionized
the creation of feasible components in fields such as nano-
photonics 10,11 and meta-optics. 12,13 Unlike traditional forward 
modeling methods, which depend on iterative simulations to 
meet target responses, ID methods simplify the process by 
reversing the problem, starting from the output to identify the 
best input configuration.
The ID approach has increasingly adopted machine learning 
(ML)-assisted methods, providing more versatile and scalable 
frameworks for quick and effective designs. 14 ML has become 
a powerful tool for both forward modeling and inverse design 
because it can grasp the complexity and nonlinear relationships 
in physical systems. 15–17 Its success has been demonstrated 
across a wide range of optical components. 18–21 By learning from 
data or the governing physics equations, machine learning mod-
els can speed up the design process significantly while maintain-
ing high accuracy. For example, the ML method improved 
model analysis accuracy by lowering the error to around 0.7% 
compared with commercial solvers. 22 Similarly, ML-based ID 
for mode-selective couplers achieved mode conversion efficien-
cies of over 93%. 23 These capabilities make ML not only an 
enabler for designing individual photonic components but also 
a key element in the development of inverse system design, 
where multiple optical components are co-optimized within 
an integrated framework. Figure 1 further illustrates this concept 
and serves as the main focus of this review, where, for a specific 
photonic application, the overall system can be broken down 
into basic optical elements, each of which can be optimized 
independently using ML-driven inverse design methods. 
Hence, in this work, we present a comprehensive review of 
the ID of key photonic devices and components, with an

emphasis on ML-driven approaches developed since 2019. 
Various ML architectures, including convolutional, recurrent, 
physics-informed, and neural networks, have been employed 
to design and optimize active and passive optical components 
such as light sources, amplifiers, passive elements (e.g., cou-
plers, splitters, and gratings), optical fibers, and metamaterial 
absorbers for optical detection applications. We systematically 
analyze these advancements, highlighting key methods, ma-
chine learning algorithms, design goals, and achieved results. 
By emphasizing ML-driven inverse design approaches, this
review aims to offer insights into the capabilities, challenges, 
and future outlook of data-driven photonics design.
Figure 2 shows the organization of the review in graphical 
form. Section 2 outlines the research methodology used in this 
review, whereas Sec. 3 provides an overview of both traditional 
and ML-based techniques commonly applied in the inverse de-
sign of photonic components. Section 4 covers recent advances 
in the inverse design of optical sources, including ML-assisted 
inverse designs of lasers and light-emitting diodes (LEDs). 
Section 5 discusses optical amplifiers, including Raman, semi-
conductor, and fiber amplifiers. Section 6 discusses passive 
optical components, such as power splitters, gratings, and 
mode-selective couplers. Section 7 reviews ML-enabled design 
strategies for various types of optical fibers. Section 8 focuses 
on the inverse design of metamaterial absorbers (MMAs) for 
optical detection applications. Last, Sec. 9 outlines the present 
status, key challenges, and future research directions toward 
the practical realization of advanced optical systems, and the 
concluding remarks are summarized in Sec. 10.
2 Review Methodology
This section presents the systematic approach adopted for iden-
tifying, selecting, and analyzing the relevant literature on the 
machine learning driven ID of optical devices and components. 
The methodology follows the Preferred Reporting Items for 
Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) framework 
and is illustrated in Fig. 3.
The process began with searches across major scientific 
databases, including Scopus, Google Scholar, IEEE Xplore, 
ScienceDirect, and the Optica Publishing Group, covering 
publications from 2019 to November 2025. The initial search 
was conducted using the query: “Photonic inverse design 
and machine learning.” The results included peer-reviewed jour-
nal articles and conference proceedings. Next, additional papers 
were identified using a combination of keywords related to both 
machine learning (e.g., machine learning, deep learning, 
reinforcement learning, artificial neural network, and convolu-
tional neural network) and photonic inverse design (e.g., inverse 
design, photonic devices, semiconductor lasers, fiber lasers, 
Raman amplifiers, semiconductor optical amplifiers, fiber am-

Following the database search, duplicate and out-of-scope 
entries were removed. The remaining articles were screened 
to exclude those that did not address either inverse design or 
data-driven optimization for optical devices and components. 
The shortlisted papers were then thoroughly reviewed, and stud-
ies that did not explicitly link ML models with the inverse de-
sign of these optical elements were excluded. Finally, 65 papers 
were included in this review, which are categorized into five 
classes: optical sources, optical amplifiers, passive optical com-
ponents, optical fibers, and optical detectors.
3 Background of Inverse Design 
Approaches
In photonics, ID involves identifying the optimal physical struc-
ture of a device that will produce a desired optical response. 
Unlike traditional forward design, which begins with a prede-
fined geometry and analyzes its behavior, ID reverses the pro-
cess by retrieving the ideal design from a specified performance 
target. The physics governing a photonic structure is described 
by Maxwell’s equations, which model the interaction of light 
and matter. These fundamental equations are numerically solved 
using simulation techniques such as the finite-difference time-
domain (FDTD) and finite-element method (FEM), enabling ac-
curate analysis of electromagnetic wave propagation and device 
behavior. By coupling these simulation methods with optimiza-
tion algorithms, ID facilitates the automated design of complex 
and highly functional photonic devices—including photonic 
crystals, 24–26 metamaterials, 27–29 and plasmonic devices, 30,31 thus 
extending beyond the limitations of conventional design ap-
proaches to unlock novel optical functionalities. In this section, 
we discuss conventional optimization techniques as well as 
machine-learning-based methods that are commonly used to 
achieve the desired functional characteristics in ID, and a typical 
categorization chart is presented in Fig. 4.
3.1 Conventional Optimization Approaches for ID
Conventional optimization approaches in ID rely on iterative 
search algorithms that explore the design space and adjust 
structural configurations to optimize light–matter interactions. 
Unlike direct analytical approaches, which become infeasible 
for complex photonic structures and components, these optimi-
zation algorithms provide a systematic way to navigate the 
vast parameter space and identify high-performance designs. 
However, due to the highly nonlinear and high-dimensional 
nature of photonic optimization problems, these methods often 
require extensive computational resources. 
One widely used approach is the genetic algorithm (GA), 
which is inspired by the principles of natural evolution. GA 
operates by maintaining a population of candidate solutions, 
which undergo selection, crossover, and mutation to evolve 
toward better designs over multiple generations. For optimiz-
ing photonic devices with unconventional geometries, such as 
metamaterials 32 and nanoparticles, 33 this method has been spe-
cifically proven effective. By exploring a wide range of feasible 
solutions, GA helps to achieve novel structures that a typical 
design intuition might overlook. However, despite its useful-
ness, GA can be computationally expensive as it requires recur-
sive electromagnetic simulations for fitness evaluation at 
each step. 
Particle swarm optimization (PSO) is yet another powerful 
optimization method, which mimics the collective behavior 
of swarming organisms, such as birds or fish. In this optimiza-
tion technique, each candidate solution, or “ particle,” moves 
through the design space by updating its position based on both 
its own best known solution and that of its neighbors. It enables 
an effective equilibrium between exploration and exploitation, 
making it appropriate for optimizing photonic structures such 
as waveguide couplers, 34 optical sensors, 35 and plasmonic 
devices. 36 Compared with GA, PSO usually converges faster 
as it relies on collective learning instead of random mutations. 
However, it may struggle with local optima in highly complex 
design landscapes. On the other hand, devices relying on binary 
design parameters, such as pixelated diffractive optics, holo-
graphic metasurfaces, and direct binary search (DBS), 37 provide 
an effective solution at a cost of high computational complexity. 
DBS iteratively modifies individual pixels in a design and 
evaluates their impact on optical performance, gradually refin-
ing the structure to maximize efficiency. 38 Although DBS can 
attain highly optimized designs for binary systems, the inher-
ent brute-force nature makes it a bottleneck for high-resolution 
configurations due to the exponential growth of the search
space.
Despite their widespread use and success in the ID paradigm,
these optimization techniques suffer from solving the entire op-
timization problem again from scratch, for every new configu-
ration. Thus, making it less popular as the process becomes 
computationally expensive, particularly for complex photonic 
structures with large parameter spaces. These limitations have 
driven interest in ML-assisted ID, which leverages data-driven 
approaches to accelerate optimization and widen the design 
space of feasible photonic structures. Unlike conventional 
methods, ML shifts much of the computational effort to an off-
line training phase. Although the training phase can be a 
resource-intensive task, the inference stage is extremely 
quick, making it popular for real-time and large-scale design 
exploration.

3.2 Machine Learning Approaches for ID
Machine learning has revolutionized ID for photonic devices 
by overcoming the limitations of conventional optimization-
based inverse design methods. Machine learning utilizes data to 
efficiently explore vast design spaces to discover complex, non-
intuitive photonic structures with greater flexibility. In this sec-
tion, we will describe the ML techniques that are mostly used in 
ID. These include artificial neural networks (ANNs), convolu-
tional neural networks (CNNs), recurrent neural networks 
(RNNs), and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs). These 
are among the most widely deployed techniques in the photon-
ics domain and have been successfully applied to the design and 
optimization of optical sources, optical amplifiers, passive com-
ponents, and optical fibers—applications that will be discussed 
in detail in the following sections.
Although many ML techniques have been successfully used 
for ID, the manner in which these techniques are integrated within 
inverse design workflows varies significantly. To provide a coher-
ent structure for our review, we organize ML-driven ID methods 
into five paradigms based on the main role played by ML: (i) for-
ward surrogates, (ii) inverse models, (iii) tandem architectures, 
(iv) physics-guided approaches, and (v) reinforcement learning. 
This paradigm-oriented view is also presented in Figs. 1 and 4. 
This perspective helps unify diverse methodologies and provides 
useful insights about how ML techniques address the most 
common challenges of inverse design, which include the unique-
ness problem, computational efficiency, and data constraints. 
As evident from Figs. 1 and 4, these paradigms do not cor-
respond to mutually exclusive sets of ML methods. A single 
architecture, such as a multilayer perceptron (MLP) or a CNN, 
may appear across multiple paradigms, with its role defined not 
by its structure but by how it is employed within the inverse 
design problem. Once trained, the forward surrogate paradigm 
can employ various ML models to predict electromagnetic char-
acteristics of devices instead of performing computationally ex-
pensive simulations. These predictions then become the input to 
the optimization algorithms to search the design space effi-
ciently to come up with designs quickly. The inverse model 
paradigm directly learns the mapping from desired optical re-
sponses to device geometries. On the other hand, Tandem archi-
tectures combine both forward and inverse networks in a closed-
loop fashion where the forward model ensures physical consis-
tency of designs generated by the inverse model. Physics-guided 
approaches try to introduce the physical laws into the ML train-
ing process through physics-informed neural networks or using 
physics-guided loss functions. In this way, the ML models en-
sure that the underlying physical principles are satisfied in their 
solutions. Finally, the reinforcement learning paradigm frames 
inverse design as a sequential decision-making problem, where 
an agent learns to optimize device structures through trial-and-
error interactions with the electromagnetic environment. 
Previous review articles often classify ML-based inverse de-
sign methods into only two broad categories, i.e., forward and 
inverse modeling. 15–17,39–41 This overlooks important methodo-
logical differences among modern approaches. However, cate-
gorization into five paradigms provides a more detailed and 
function-oriented perspective of the ML-assisted ID landscape. 
In the following, key ML techniques used in ID are discussed.
3.2.1 Artificial neural networks
ANNs, also commonly referred to as MLP, form the founda-
tional structure upon which more advanced architectures such
as CNNs and RNNs are built. An ANN is inspired by the struc-
ture and functioning of biological neural networks. It consists of 
a layered architecture comprising an input layer, one or more 
hidden layers (HL), and an output layer. Each layer contains 
multiple interconnected nodes (or neurons) that mimic the 
behavior of biological neurons. In a typical feedforward ANN, 
the output of each layer is passed as input to the subsequent 
layer, with every neuron in one layer fully connected to all neu-
rons in the next, as shown in Fig. 5. The operation of a single 
neuron consists of summation followed by an activation func-
tion, which can be mathematically described as

where x i is the input features, w i is the corresponding weights, 
b is the bias, and ϕ is the activation function, such as the

error is then propagated backward through the network using 
the backpropagation algorithm, and the weights and biases are 
updated via gradient descent to minimize this error.
ANNs provide a powerful platform to solve complex prob-
lems in the ID paradigm. 14,42–46 In ID applications, ANNs are 
typically employed in two complementary modes (shown in 
Fig. 6): forward modeling and inverse design. In the forward 
modeling approach, an ANN is trained to learn the mapping 
from structural parameters such as geometry, material proper-
ties, and layer thicknesses to the corresponding optical response, 
which may include transmission spectra, reflection coefficients, 
or mode profiles. The trained model allows for rapid prediction 
of a device’s optical behavior without the need for computation-
ally intensive full-wave simulations. 47,48 By contrast, the ID 
paradigm aims to identify the optimal structural parameters that 
will yield a desired optical response. This can be achieved by 
training a separate ANN model that learns the inverse mapping 
or using the forward model in conjunction with conventional 
optimization techniques to iteratively search for the input struc-
ture that reproduces the target response. Together, these two 
paradigms enable efficient exploration of the design space: the 
forward model provides fast evaluations, whereas the inverse 
model enables the generation of novel photonic designs tailored 
to specific performance criteria.

Studies have shown that ANNs can outperform traditional 
methods such as FDTD and FEM solvers, reducing computation 
time to just a few milliseconds while maintaining comparable 
accuracy to commercial simulation tools. 49,50 However, although 
simple feedforward ANNs are effective across many problem 
domains, they may struggle with high-dimensional spatial data 
or temporal sequences. These limitations have led to the devel-
opment of specialized architectures—such as CNNs and 
RNNs—which are better suited for handling image-based 
and sequential data, respectively. A detailed discussion of these 
neural networks is presented in the subsequent sections.
3.2.2 Convolutional neural networks
A CNN is a deep learning (DL) architecture that has gained pop-
ularity due to its remarkable performance in the field of com-
puter vision and image processing. 51 The capabilities of CNNs 
for ID have been exploited to develop novel architectures aimed 
at improving the efficiency of photonic devices. A CNN extracts 
spatial features from input data, such as device geometries, and 
maps them to optical properties. It consists of multiple layers 
that perform a combination of linear and nonlinear operations 
to process and transform the input into a meaningful output.
There are three key components in a typical CNN architec-
ture: convolution, activation, and pooling. A convolutional layer 
performs a linear convolution operation, which is the core of 
CNNs. In this layer, the network extracts features by sliding ker-
nels over the input data. The output is the result of the sum of 
the element-wise product between the input data and the kernel 
at each position. This operation is mathematically expressed as

Next, a nonlinear activation function is applied to the output 
of the convolution operation. The most commonly used function 
is the ReLU, 52 defined as
ReLUðxÞ ¼ maxð0; xÞ: 
(3)
The ReLU function retains only the most relevant features by 
setting all negative values to zero, thereby introducing nonlin-
earity and improving training efficiency. A pooling operation is 
then applied to reduce the spatial dimensions of the feature 
maps. This operation simplifies the output and reduces the 
number of learnable parameters, helping to prevent overfitting. 
Max pooling is a widely used method, where each feature map is 
divided into nonoverlapping regions, and only the maximum 
value from each region is retained. 53
In a typical CNN architecture, these operations are repeated 
across multiple layers. The output of one layer serves as the in-
put to the next, gradually building hierarchical feature represen-
tations. The final layer is typically fully connected, similar to the 
layer in an ANN, and produces the network’s output, which is 
compared with the ground truth to compute the loss. The CNN 
then updates its kernels and weights via backpropagation, 
optimizing performance through iterative learning. A visual 
representation of the complete CNN architecture, highlighting 
its core components and hierarchical structure, is provided in 
Fig. 7.
CNNs have been successfully applied to various photonic 
devices, enhancing both their design efficiency and perfor-
mance. For instance, metasurfaces 47,54 have been optimized 
using CNNs to predict transmission and reflection spectra,

significantly improving the accuracy of wavefront shaping. 
Similarly, in silicon photonic waveguides, 55,56 CNN-based net-
works have been employed to optimize mode conversion effi-
ciency, allowing improved light confinement and decreasing 
loss of propagation. Another important application is in plas-
monic nanostructures, 57 where a CNN has been used in design-
ing precise wavelength resonance structures, improving spectral 
selectivity and tunability. In a recent work, 58 CNNs have been 
engaged in the ID of Ag nanohole arrays by precisely predicting 
their structural parameters from the target transmission spectrum 
and structural color ðR 2 ≈ 0.99Þ, allowing fast optimization for 
surface-enhanced Raman scattering and high-resolution nanoscale 
color printing. Compared with typical optimizing algorithms that 
are iterative in nature, which require enormous simulations as well 
as manual tuning of parameters, CNNs learn complex mappings 
between device geometry and optical performance through super-
vised learning. This enables them to directly predict optimal or 
near-optimal designs with minimal computation while bypassing 
iterative solvers during inference. Thus, convergence is achieved 
in one computational step, and designers can quickly explore a 
broader range of geometries and performance trade-offs, facilitat-
ing efficient optimization over high-dimensional and nonintuitive 
design spaces. These advancements demonstrate how CNNs revo-
lutionize photonic inverse design, reducing computational costs 
while achieving superior device performance and opening path-
ways to novel photonic architectures.
3.2.3 Recurrent neural networks
RNNs are specialized in modeling sequential dependencies of 
data by maintaining a memory of previous inputs. It is a class 
of DL architectures particularly well-suited for predicting time-
series or sequential data. 59 The uniqueness of this network lies in 
the hidden layers and feedback loops, which enable the network 
to retain information from prior inputs and use it to enhance 
performance on current and future predictions. This looping 
mechanism effectively introduces a form of memory into the 
network over time. Owing to this capability, RNNs are espe-
cially effective in applications involving time series predic-
tion, 60,61 speech recognition, 62 and natural language processing. 
Figure 8(a) shows the visual representation of the basic RNN 
architecture. The input, output, and the underlying hidden layers 
are represented by x, y, and h, respectively. Depending upon the 
number of layers, all these states (x, y, and h) can be expressed 
at any time instance t. The variables W xh , W hh , and W yh are the 
hidden weight matrices, which vary in each timestamp. The hid-
den state h t at time step t is computed using the current input x t 
and the previous hidden state h t−1 as follows:
h t ¼ aðW xh x t þ W hh h t−1 Þ: 
(4)
The RNN takes sequential input data in vector form to com-
pute the hidden states using the activation function. The activa-
tion function (a), such as the hyperbolic tangent function (tanh), 
introduces nonlinearity and ensures the values remain within a 
bounded range. The use of shared weights across time steps al-
lows RNNs to generalize across varying input sequence lengths. 
The output is evaluated using the hidden-layer states via soft-
max or any other suitable function. Figure 8(b) illustrates the 
unrolled architectural flow of a basic RNN, where sequential 
inputs are processed through recurrent connections that main-
tain temporal dependencies.
Despite their conceptual simplicity, RNNs face challenges 
such as vanishing or exploding gradients when modeling long 
sequences. To address this, advanced variants such as long 
short-term memory (LSTM) networks and gated recurrent units 
are often used. 63 These architectures introduce gating mecha-
nisms to better capture long-range dependencies in sequences. 
Similar to CNNs, RNNs are trained using backpropagation 
through time, where the model’s weights are updated based 
on the gradient of the loss function with respect to the predicted 
outputs across the sequence.
RNNs have been successfully applied in ID of photonic de-
vices, significantly improving optimization efficiency compared 
with conventional iterative algorithms. Yan et al. 64 demonstrated 
the usefulness of RNN by proposing an RNN-based model to 
accurately learn the sequential characteristics of optical spectra 
to achieve the inverse design of nanophotonic structures. For 
nanorod hyperbolic metamaterials, this RNN-based model 
showed high accuracy in predicting spectra. With a mean rela-
tive error of 0.32%, the proposed model is capable of predicting 
an unknown spectrum based on a known spectrum.
Similarly, an LSTM-based RNN combined with gradient-
free optimization has been employed to accelerate the inverse 
design of dispersive optical devices, achieving up to 99.62% 
accuracy in predicting group velocity dispersion and over 
1000× speedup compared with conventional simulations. This 
approach enables efficient dispersion optimization in both fiber 
and waveguides. 65 Bidirectional RNNs have also been effec-
tively applied in the ID of high-speed optical communication 
systems, enabling the mitigation of transmission impairments 
such as Kerr-induced nonlinearities and dispersion-induced 
power fading. By inverse mapping between degraded signals 
and their undistorted forms, these post-detection RNN tech-
niques provide power-efficient and adaptive solutions for 
real-time signal restoration, supporting mid-term deployment 
in both long-haul and short-reach networks. 66 Compared with 
iterative optimization algorithms, which require multiple costly 
electromagnetic simulations, RNNs learn from data and general-
ize across different design configurations, enabling faster con-
vergence and reduced computational overhead. In addition, 
iterative methods often get trapped in local minima, whereas 
RNNs leverage historical information to navigate complex de-
sign spaces more effectively, making them particularly advanta-
geous for real-time photonic optimization and adaptive device 
tuning.
3.2.4 Physics-informed neural networks
The neural networks discussed so far rely heavily on datasets for 
training the underlying model. As a result, their performance 
and generalization are highly dependent on the availability,

quality, and diversity of training data. In many photonics 
applications, generating such datasets—either through simula-
tions or experiments—can be computationally expensive, time-
consuming, or practically infeasible. Furthermore, purely data-
driven models often lack physical interpretability and may 
fail to generalize well beyond the training distribution. To over-
come these limitations, PINNs have been introduced by Raissi 
et al. 67 as a promising alternative. PINNs can utilize any ANN 
architecture by incorporating a priori physical knowledge— 
such as Maxwell’s equations—into the training process. These 
governing equations embedded directly into the loss function 
guide the model to learn solutions that inherently satisfy the 
underlying physics. This allows PINNs to make accurate predic-
tions even with limited training data, making them particularly 
effective for inverse design tasks where acquiring large datasets 
can be challenging.
A schematic of PINN architecture is depicted in Fig. 9. PINN 
loss function combines both the data term, used when training 
data are available, and the physics-based term that ensures 
compliance with the governing equations. The complete loss 
function is typically defined as
L ¼ L physics þ L IC∕BC þ L data ;
(5)
where L physics enforces the governing differential equations by 
minimizing the residuals computed via differentiation methods. 
L IC∕BC penalizes deviations from known initial or boundary 
conditions, and L data measures the discrepancy between the net-
work’s predictions and observed or simulated data. It is impor-
tant to note that L data is an optional term, included only when 
additional labeled data are available to further guide the training 
process. Notably, L physics functions as an unsupervised loss 
term, allowing the model to train on collocation points in the 
domain without the need for labeled data. This hybrid approach 
enables PINNs to produce solutions that are both physically 
consistent and data-informed. The training process ensures that 
network predictions inherently satisfy fundamental physical 
laws, significantly enhancing model accuracy even in the pres-
ence of sparse data. Compared with purely data-driven models, 
PINNs generalize more effectively to unseen conditions and 
offer continuous, mesh-free solutions for complex inverse prob-
lems in photonics.
Despite their potential in improving both the efficiency and 
accuracy of photonic device design, the practical implementation
of PINNs in optical inverse problems remains relatively limited— 
highlighting substantial opportunities for further development in 
real-world photonic systems and device fabrication workflows. 
In Refs. 68–70, the authors have applied PINNs to find accurate 
solutions of the wave equation. Zhang et al. 71 explored the use 
of PINNs to study the extended higher-order nonlinear 
Schrödinger equation (NLSE) in optical fibers, which models 
ultrashort pulse propagation considering third-order and fourth-
order dispersion terms. PINNs were employed to derive numeri-
cal solutions for one-soliton, two-soliton, and rogue wave dy-
namics governed by the NLSE. A key contribution was the 
data-driven discovery of unknown coefficients within the 
NLSE using PINNs, even with noisy or limited data. The frame-
work leverages the universal approximation theorem, where the 
PINN learns shared parameters (weights and biases) by mini-
mizing a loss function that includes residuals of the governing 
physical equations.
With the foundation of ML-assisted inverse system design, the 
following sections examine its implementation across a range of 
photonic devices and components, highlighting how these tech-
niques are tailored to address specific design challenges.
4 Inverse Design of Optical Sources
Designing optical sources such as semiconductor lasers, LEDs, 
and fiber lasers requires managing complex interactions be-
tween optical fields and material properties within device struc-
tures. Traditional design approaches depend on experimental 
iterations or computationally intensive simulations. Extracting 
device parameters or optimizing structures for desired per-
formance is challenging due to fabrication variability and the 
existence of multiple parameter combinations that produce sim-
ilar outcomes. In recent years, inverse design methodologies, 
greatly accelerated by ML, have emerged as powerful tools 
to overcome these challenges, enabling faster, automated, and 
optimal designs. 72–81 This section discusses recent developments 
in the ML-driven inverse design applied to optical sources and 
summarizes them in Table 1.
4.1 Semiconductor Lasers and LEDs
ID of semiconductor lasers and LEDs primarily focuses on 
extracting device parameters and tailoring/optimizing the per-
formance. In 2020, Ma et al. 72 proposed a hybrid approach com-
bining a deep-learning neural network (DLNN) with PSO for 
inverse design of a semiconductor laser. The DLNN is trained 
on simulation data from a traveling wave model (TWM), which 
accurately predicts laser output power and small-signal re-
sponses, achieving a low mean square error (MSE) of 
∼2 × 10 −3 . This DLNN-PSO framework successfully extracted 
device parameters matching the desired characteristics and 
achieved a remarkable speedup, 14,106 times faster than using 
the TWM with PSO alone. Later, next year, Kim et al. 73 em-
ployed deep neural networks (DNNs) to optimize the optical 
properties of multilayer organic LEDs (OLEDs). A forward net-
work was trained on 260,000 samples generated using simula-
tions to predict light extraction efficiency (LEE) from structural 
parameters such as layer thicknesses and refractive index (RI), 
achieving a root mean square error (RMSE) of 4.42 × 10 −3 and 
an inference time of ∼0.6 μs per structure using a graphics 
processing unit (GPU). Two inverse design approaches were ex-
plored: a backward NN (Bwd) trained in a tandem configuration 
and a neural network-assisted genetic algorithm (NNGA).

Very recently, Niu et al. 74 proposed a DL-based inverse de-
sign method for distributed feedback (DFB) lasers by integrat-
ing a DNN with a generative adversarial network (GAN), as 
shown in Figs. 10(a) and 10(b), aiming to improve the accuracy 
and efficiency of parameter extraction from laser output charac-

extracting nine intrinsic parameters from simulated optical

as PSO combined with CNNs. By training on data generated 
via numerical simulation, the tandem network is developed, 
where a pretrained GAN simulates forward laser behavior 
and a DNN learns to invert laser responses to retrieve physical 
parameters. The results showed a dramatic reduction in relative 
average error down to 1.15 × 10 −4 compared with 1.73 × 10 −2

4.2 Fiber Lasers
Since 2020, several studies have applied machine learning to the 
design of mode-locked fiber lasers (MLFLs) to control the pulse 
shapes and their evolution in optical fibers. A summary of these 
studies is provided in Table 1. Boscolo et al. 75 employed a 
supervised neural network trained on simulation data from the 
NLSE to model pulse shaping. The network retrieved propaga-
tion parameters—fiber length, soliton number, and gain/loss— 
required to produce desired pulse shapes and was also used
for inverse design to classify the initial pulse corresponding 
to a given output. As solving the NLSE numerically via the 
split-step Fourier method is computationally expensive, faster 
alternatives have been explored. To generate pulses with specific 
characteristics: duration, energy, and shape, Fang et al. 76 pro-
posed a data-driven ID approach combining an LSTM model 
with deep reinforcement learning (RL) using the deep deter-
ministic policy gradient (DDPG) algorithm. The DDPG learned 
to adjust cavity parameters by minimizing the discrepancy 
between predicted and target pulses, successfully designing 
MLFLs that generate target femtosecond solitons, as shown 
in Figs. 11(a) and 11(b), converging in under 1.3 h on a GPU. 
Recently, Kasmi et al. 79 introduced an ANN-based inverse 
design framework for figure-eight fiber lasers operating in the 
dissipative soliton resonance regime. A fully connected neural 
network (FCNN), trained on NLSE-generated data, learned a 
direct mapping from a reduced set of temporal pulse-shape 
descriptors (12 features sampled along the leading edge) to 
the four key amplifier parameters of the dual-amplifier cavity: 
the small-signal gains and saturation energies of EDFA1 and 
EDFA2. The dataset, constructed from over 1500 numerical 
simulations, enabled the network to predict cavity parameters 
that yield rectangular or Gaussian pulses without requiring 
additional NLSE simulations. Using these ANN-predicted para-
meters, the authors validated their results in OptiSystem, dem-
onstrating strong agreement with the target pulse shapes, with 
relative errors of less than 0.3%. The study highlights the effi-
ciency of ANN-based solvers in accelerating the design of com-
plex fiber-laser cavities. It demonstrates that the framework can 
be extended to real-time or adaptive laser optimization. 
Unlike earlier works, which focus on the time domain, Luo 
et al. 77 applied ID to control intracavity dispersion to achieve 
the desired output spectra of MLFLs in the wavelength domain.

4.3 Photonic Crystal Lasers
Inverse design has been applied to optimize the photonic crystal 
(PC) laser and cavity. Although Table 1 summarizes only the 
works on lasers, here, we include a discussion of inverse design 
of a laser cavity as it constitutes an essential component of a 
laser system. For instance, Li et al. 80 applied inverse design us-
ing deep RL to achieve target quality factor (Q factor), modal 
volume, and resonance wavelength. Learning to design optical-
resonators (L2DO) framework was introduced, leveraging RL to 
perform inverse design of nanophotonic laser cavities without 
prior knowledge. Two algorithms were used in L2DO: (1) deep 
Q-learning by Google DeepMind and (2) proximal policy opti-
mization (PPO) by OpenAI. L2DO was initialized with design 
parameters and corresponding optical responses and then tasked 
with finding design parameters to match target outputs. In an-
other report, Zhang et al. 81 applied inverse design to PC surface-
emitting lasers (PCSELs), modeling it as a sequential decision-
making problem via the PCSEL inverse design transformer 
(PiT) framework. PiT, based on a decision transformer (DT) ar-
chitecture, leverages past trajectories and current states to pre-
dict actions. Trained on an offline dataset of 16,057 samples, 
PiT outperformed a behavior cloning baseline, which involves 
training a network to imitate a demonstrated dataset, effectively
using offline data to achieve target designs without further online 
interaction. In this approach, however, the quality of the offline 
dataset significantly influences the inverse design performance.
4.4 Failure Modes and Open Problems—Optical 
Sources
• Simulation–experiment gap: Many models depend on 
idealized simulations, neglecting fabrication and experimental 
variations. Including uncertainty and tolerance modeling is 
crucial for ensuring robustness.
• Limited objectives: Optimization often focuses on a single 
metric, such as color shift. To achieve more realistic designs, 
frameworks that handle multiple objectives and fabrication con-
siderations are necessary.
• Nonunique mapping: Multiple geometries can produce the 
same spectra. Incorporating richer spectral data and models such 
as INNs or CNNs may help minimize this ambiguity.
• Restricted operating space: Existing models assume ideal 
pulses or steady conditions. Incorporating factors such as chirp, 
gain saturation, and noise could enhance practical applicability.
5 Inverse Design of Optical Amplifiers
The drive for research into UWB systems for optical commu-
nications, as well as ISAC technologies, is expected to span 
wide wavelength bands across the electromagnetic spectrum, 
covering O-to-U bands. Hence, RAs, 82–92 hybrid erbium-doped 
fiber amplifiers (EDFAs)-RA, 93,94 and semiconductor optical 
amplifiers (SOAs) 95 are being considered promising candidates 
due to their potential for broad wavelength coverage and gain 
profile flexibility. Moreover, RAs employing nonconventional 
fibers, such as few-mode fibers (FMFs), have also been inves-
tigated for potential application in mode-division multiplex-
ing (MDM) communication systems. 96–99 ML-driven NNs have 
emerged as a powerful tool for the ID of optical amplifiers, aim-
ing to directly determine the required amplifier parameters (e.g., 
pump powers, wavelengths, and structural parameters) to 
achieve a desired performance target (e.g., a specific gain pro-
file, noise figure, or power evolution). Very recently, this ap-
proach has also penetrated 
into designing narrow-band 
ytterbium-doped fiber amplifier (YDFA) as well. 100 Hence, such 
tools are expected to play a pivotal role in the design of future 
UWB and ISAC infrastructures. In the following, we provide a 
comprehensive review of ML-assisted ID of optical amplifiers 
from the literature while summarizing the key features of these 
reports in Table 2.

5.1 Raman Amplifiers (RAs)
Several studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of ML 
frameworks, often based on feed-forward NNs, for the inverse 
design of RA. 82,83,86,90,93,96,99 Early work focused on learning the 
mapping from a target gain profile (e.g., arbitrary, flat, or tilted 
shapes across the C-band or C+L band) to the necessary pump 
powers and/or wavelengths. 82,83,96 These frameworks typically 
involve training two NNs: an inverse NN (INN), also referred 
to as a backward NN (Bwd), to predict the pump configuration 
from the target gain, and sometimes a forward NN (Fwd) to pre-
dict the gain from a given pump configuration. 83,90,96 A typical 
schematic plan, Bwd architecture, and the ID results of an RA 
are shown in Figs. 12(a)–12(c), respectively. Besides, experi-
mental validations across various fiber types affirm the ability 
of these ML models to predict pump powers for arbitrary C-
band gain profiles with high accuracy, often achieving maxi-
mum errors (MaxErr) < 0.5 dB for 80% of cases. 96
To improve the precision of the initial prediction of Bwd, a 
common strategy involves a subsequent fine-optimization 
step. 83,96 This often uses the Fwd within a gradient descent al-
gorithm to iteratively refine the pump parameters, minimizing 
the error between the predicted and target gain profiles. 83,90 This 
fine-tuning significantly improves accuracy, especially for cases 
where the initial Bwd prediction is less precise or for challeng-
ing target profiles such as flat gains, reducing MaxErr to 0.35 dB 
for experimental data. 96 Moreover, a further refinement to early 
models by incorporating the input signal power spectral density 
information as additional inputs to the NNs, Moura et al. dem-
onstrated improved robustness, accurate pump predictions even 
with varying input signal conditions. 86,93 MaxErr < 0.5 dB was 
reported when tested experimentally on different types of 
fiber. These ML approaches offer substantial benefits in speed 
and complexity compared with traditional methods, providing
near-instantaneous pump configurations once the NNs are 
trained.
Both approaches of using gain profile in the wavelength 
domain as well as in the frequency domain 87,94,98 have been 
reported in the literature for the ML-driven ID. In addition, be-
yond gain profile shaping, ML models have been developed to 
simultaneously predict the noise figure (NF) along with the 
pump configuration for a target gain profile. 87 This provides 
a more comprehensive design tool as NF is a critical parameter 
for system performance, and experimental validation demon-
strated high accuracy, with MaxErr < 0.5 dB for both gain and 
NF predictions.
For UWB amplification covering S-C-L bands, NNs have 
been successfully engaged to design RA following dual ap-
proaches. Although the first predicts the pump currents required 
to achieve specific loss profiles, 84 the second estimates the re-
quired pump powers for given gain profiles. 99 The experimental 
results showed good precision, with RMSE < 0.74 dB for loss 
profiles and <13% error for the prediction of pump currents, 
enabling precise control over UWB spectra. 84 Similar techniques 
were applied to optimize a hybrid EDFA-RA system, focusing 
on adjusting only the Raman pump currents (12 pumps) while 
keeping the EDFA operation fixed, 94 or both the EDFA and the 
Raman pump powers, 93 to flatten the overall gain profile of the C 
+ L band. Although the former report highlighted a significant 
reduction in gain ripple from 6.7 to 1.9 dB, using the gradient 
descent algorithm (GDA) fine optimization technique, the latter 
showed an RMSE of 6.9 mW in predicting pump powers. 
Very recently, ML has been applied to inverse-design of 
RA in hollow-core fibers (HCFs), aimed at UWB, multiband 
optical transmission, 92 and the summary of this work is high-
lighted in Table 2. Leveraging an INN for global exploration 
and a feed-forward NN for local tuning, the method efficiently 
determines pump configurations to target specific gain profiles.

The experimental results are shown in Figs. 13(a) and 13(b), 
where an RMSE of 0.58 is achieved for an 18 dB gain level. 
Moreover, further demonstration of a 25 Gbaud 64 quadrature 
amplitude modulation transmission system over a 10 km HCF 
yielded 18 dB gain level with 2 dB variation across the spec-
trum, 92 representing a significant advancement toward opti-
mized UWB networks.
Space division multiplexing (SDM), in the form of MDM, 
which exploits the spatial diversity of modes in FMFs, has also 
been envisioned as a promising communication system for 
next-generation infrastructures. In this regard, ML-driven in-
verse design of RA with FMFs has also been investigated in the 
literature. 97,98 In this case, the challenge is not only to achieve 
flatness across the wavelength band but also to minimize mode-
dependent gain (MDG). ML techniques, including NNs, have 
been used to optimize pump wavelengths, powers, and 
crucially, the power distribution among different pump modes 
to achieve intelligent gain flattening with low MDG. A gain 
flatness of <1.0 dB over the C-band with MDG fluctuations 
<0.63 dB has been reported, thus significantly outperforming 
the traditional optimization techniques such as GA. 97 More ad-
vanced approaches utilized unsupervised learning with auto-
encoder (AE) architectures combined with a differentiable 
Raman model derived from the governing equations. 98 This 
model-aware DL approach avoids the need for large pre-com-
puted datasets by directly training on ideal target gain profiles 
(flat and tilted), efficiently predicting optimal pump parameters 
for FMF RAs with good accuracy in terms of RMSE, flatness, 
and achieving an MDG of <0.02 dB. 98
ML techniques have been engaged in designing specific two-
dimensional (2D) power evolution profiles along the fiber span 
(in both frequency and distance domains), 85,88,89 which help 
mitigate nonlinear impairments and optimize SNRs. Given 
the increased complexity associated with 2D profile design, 
frameworks combining CNNs with optimization algorithms 
such as differential evolution (DE) have been proposed. The 
CNN learns the complex mapping from the 2D profile (treated 
as an image) to the pump parameters, providing a good initial 
guess for the DE algorithm, which then performs a gradient-
free optimization to fine-tune the pump powers and minimize 
a target-specific cost function (e.g., minimizing maximum 
power excursion for flat profiles or asymmetry for symmetric 
profiles). 88,89 Experimental and simulation results validated these
approaches, achieving low errors (mean absolute error, MAE 
<0.5 dB for CNN prediction 88 ) and demonstrating successful 
design of broadband 2D flat and symmetric profiles with con-
trolled power excursions and asymmetry.
Recently, ML has also been applied in the inverse design of 
RA in the U-band, where conventional amplifiers are lacking. 91 
In this case, NNs such as bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) have 
been employed for the ID of the pump’s spectral shape that 
employs broad incoherent pumps. By optimizing the pump 
spectrum, Lan et al. demonstrated both theoretically and exper-
imentally the potential to equalize optical power variations 
across 20 Gbaud dual-polarization quadrature phase shift keying 
(DP-QPSK) U-band based WDM transmission over a 125 km 
fiber, achieving a received optical power variation below 1.2 dB 
in simulation, thus enabling U-band transmission experiments. 91
5.2 Semiconductor Optical Amplifiers (SOAs)
Beyond RAs, ML-based ID has been applied to SOAs. 95 Here, 
the goal is often to determine the structural or material param-
eters of the SOA needed to achieve desired performance char-
acteristics, such as a specific gain curve or saturation power. 
One approach combines PSO with NNs. A forward NN model 
is first trained to rapidly predict SOA performance (e.g., cur-
rent–gain curve, saturation output power) based on physical 
parameters (like injection efficiency, reflectances, cavity loss, 
and dimensions). 95 This fast NN predictor is then used within 
the PSO algorithm to efficiently search the parameter space and 
find multiple sets of SOA parameters that satisfy the target per-
formance curves, achieving high accuracy with PSO fitness con-
verging at < 2 × 10 −4 . The predicted gain versus injection 
current, along with the original (target) values and the corre-
sponding error, are depicted in Fig. 13(c). On the other hand, 
the SOA gain and the output optical power as a function of input 
optical power at a fixed injection current of 350 mA are plotted 
in Fig. 13(d). The results exhibit very high accuracy of close to 
0, and the ML-based ID has been shown to significantly im-
prove the speed compared with traditional design methods, 
which rely on slower physical models.

the signal and the doped fiber area), settling to 0.1 and 0.9 after 
∼20,000 iterations, as shown in Fig. 14(b). This capability marks 
a significant advancement for continuous system diagnostics.
5.4 Failure Modes and Open Problems—Optical 
Amplifiers
• Narrow design scope: Expanding inverse design to cover 
beyond C and L bands, along with optimizing noise figure 
and throughput, would lead to more comprehensive amplifier 
solutions.
• Restricted pump tunability: Fixed-wavelength Raman 
modules restrict versatility. Using tunable or reconfigurable 
pump lasers could improve flexibility in operation.
• Prediction accuracy in nonlinear fibers: Neural networks 
work well for standard fibers, but their performance decreases 
with HNLF or IDF cases. Incorporating PINNs and more 
diverse training datasets could enhance accuracy.
• Assumed flat input spectra: Real signals are generally not 
flat. Therefore, the models require validation under realistic 
spectral conditions to ensure robustness.
6 Inverse Design of Passive Optical 
Components
ML techniques have emerged as powerful tools for the in-
verse design of photonic components, offering pathways to op-
timize complex structures and achieve desired functionalities 
with increased efficiency compared with traditional methods. 
Several studies have explored various ML frameworks to design
specific optical components such as power splitters, 19,101–106 gra-
tings, 18,107–113 and mode selective couplers (MSCs). 23,114,115 In the 
following, we provide a comprehensive review of ML-assisted 
ID of different optical components from the literature and have 
summarized the key features in Table 3.
6.1 Power/Beam Splitters
A significant area of focus has been the ID of power/beam 
splitters. Tahersima et al. 101 introduced DNNs to rapidly design
1 × 2 silicon-on-insulator (SOI) power splitters with arbitrary 
splitting ratios (ASRs) in a compact 2.6 μm × 2.6 μm footprint. 
Using a residual neural network (ResNet) to address vanishing 
gradients, the design space was constrained to a fabrication-
friendly 20 × 20 hole vectors (HV) with a fixed 45 nm etched 
hole radius [as shown in Fig. 15(a)]. Both the Fwd (to predict 
spectral responses) and the Bwd (to generate device designs) are 
trained on simulated samples, and inversely designed devices 
yielded transmission over 90% and reflection < − 20 dB across 
the 1450 to 1650 nm wavelength. Building on the 20 × 20 HV 
constraint, Tang et al. 103 extended the design space by optimiz-
ing etched hole radii within 42 to 77 nm, reduced the footprint to 
2.25 μm × 2.25 μm and extended high-efficiency (>90%) 
transmission to 1250 to 1800 nm. An adversarial conditional 
variational autoencoder (A-CVAE) was introduced with adver-
sarial censoring and active learning, enabling diverse and high-
quality geometry generation. The details of this ML architecture 
are listed in Table 3. Subsequently, Kojima et al. 104 adopted sim-
ilar design constraints (2.25 μm × 2.25 μm footprint, 40 to 
72 nm hole radii, 1250 to 1800 nm BW) and compared three 
strategies: 
forward 
modeling 
within 
optimization 
loops 
(DNN-assisted DBS), inverse modeling (avoiding external op-
timization loops), and generative modeling via A-CVAE. 
Although the DBS method accelerated optimization over con-
ventional solvers, the A-CVAE model (similar to Tang’s 103 ) pro-
duced efficient designs across various ASRs. By contrast, Banerji 
et al. 105 used a binary-additive reinforcement learning algorithm 
(b-ARLA) combining perceptron-style updates with reward-
based learning to design 50:50 T-junction beam splitters. 
Transmission of 80% was achieved in a record-small 1.2 μm × 
1.2 μm footprint by optimizing a 12 × 12 pixelated structure 
(binary pixels representing Si/air) using FDTD simulations. This 
RL-based approach departs from the supervised learning trend 
by learning through interaction without large training datasets. 
Furthermore, Dinsdale et al. 106 used DL to design universal 
optical elements by creating arbitrary transmission matrices in 
multiport multimode waveguides with weak scattering perturba-
tions. Their method employed a tandem NN to map desired 
transmission matrices to perturbation patterns and to control 
both amplitude and phase at the outputs. Although computation-
ally intensive during training data generation, the trained net-
work offered rapid ID (<50 ms). The work demonstrated ID of
1 × 2 and 3 × 3 transmission matrices and noted the network’s 
tendency to find solutions similar to training data for simpler
1 × 2 devices but generating novel solutions for complex
3 × 3 cases. Later, Deng et al. 19 designed a thermally tunable 
three-port beam splitter using an experimentally trained equiv-
alent neural network (ENN) to map heater currents to output 
powers. An inverse optimizer (Quasi-Newton method) used the 
ENN to generate heater settings for various ASRs, with the 
RMSE from model prediction and experimental validation (with 
136 experiments) remaining below 0.01 and 0.05, respectively.

Most recently, Wang et al. 102 reported ML-aided ID of di-
rectional coupler-based power splitters incorporating an arti-
ficial gauge field (AGF) waveguide structure, as depicted in 
Fig. 15(b). An NN was trained to map the relationship between 
AGF structural parameters and the transmission characteristics at 
the bar and cross ports for different ASRs. This model was used 
to inversely design broadband (1500 to 1600 nm) power splitters 
with ASRs of 5:95, 10:90, 30:70, and 50:50. Experimental val-
idation of the 5:95 and 10:90 splitters showed excellent agree-
ment with the simulation, with <0.6% and <2.8% variation in 
splitting ratio, respectively, across the 100 nm BW [depicted in 
Fig. 15(b)]. In addition, an AGF-based power splitter was incor-
porated into a microring resonator on a SiN platform, and later 
experiments demonstrated a constant extinction ratio over the 
100 nm BW.
Although all approaches offer high-efficiency and ratio-
tunable designs, Deng et al. 19 uniquely trained on experimental 
data, reducing simulation reliance but limiting adaptability to 
new setups. Generative models 103,104 better address the one-to-
many nature of inverse problems than direct inverse models, 101 
whereas RL 105 offers a data-efficient alternative well suited for 
large design spaces. Dinsdale et al. 106 expanded this landscape 
by implementing a tandem architecture and incorporating 
phase-aware transmission control in ID of universal optical 
components with broad applicability in programmable photonic 
systems. Wang et al. 102 demonstrated ML-assisted ID of power 
splitters with broadband experimental validation, highlighting 
practical viability and integration potential.
6.2 Gratings and Metagratings
Another category of optical components involves grating struc-
tures. Xu et al. 108 improved the tandem NN approach for the 
ID of grating couplers (GCs) by introducing a modified loss
function that combines the spectral MSE of the forward simu-
lation neural network (SNN) with the structural MSE of the 
INN. This enhanced accuracy by 10% to 20% over standard tan-
dem networks, achieving 96.49% for the SNN and 90.26% for 
the INN. After evaluating various activation functions, ReLU 
was chosen for both networks due to its effectiveness in avoid-
ing gradient vanishing, unlike the sigmoid. Du et al. 107 combined 
acoustic meta-gratings with WDM and applied DL for ID. They 
used a predicting neural network (PNN) with PSO to efficiently 
determine optimal grating parameters (cylinder positions and 
radii) for directing different frequencies to distinct diffraction 
orders with more than 98% efficiency, as shown in Fig. 15(c), 
and tabulated in Table 3). A second-generation PNN + PSO was 
retrained on refined data for higher accuracy, and a variational 
autoencoder (VAE)-based probabilistic ID network was intro-
duced to assess design sensitivity. This PNN + PSO method 
was preferred over tandem networks as their targets were de-
fined by dominant diffraction orders in frequency bands rather 
than precise spectra.
Moreover, Liu et al. 109 reported the ID of multilayer deep-
etched gratings (MDEG) for multispectral imaging filters using 
a DNN with an adaptive solution space. Instead of MSE, cross-
entropy (CE) loss was used to output probability distributions 
over discretized parameters. By selecting the top K candidates 
and evaluating them with a pre-trained forward model, the one-
to-many mapping problem was addressed with superior perfor-
mance and lower MSE, even with significantly less training 
data. Thereafter, Hejazi et al. 18 demonstrated the ID of a wave-
guide grating that acts as a mode converter. Three different DNNs 
were trained to predict modal scattering parameters (S 11 , S 21 , and 
S 31 ) from grating geometries. The method focuses on mode-
selective reflection via cascaded-mode gratings and uses a 
GDA-based ID algorithm applied directly to a trained forward
model. They achieved high-accuracy IDs with MSE < 1.0 × 10 −10 .

Since 2024, ML-driven efficient ID of complex fiber Bragg 
gratings (FBGs) has also garnered attention, which are crucial 
for filtering, sensing, and dispersion compensation applications. 
For instance, Yu et al. 110 combined ANNs with GAs to optimize 
aperiodic multinotch FBGs for near-infrared hydroxyl (OH) 
suppression in astronomy. The ANN acted as a forward model 
predicting spectral responses from index modulation profiles 
qðzÞ, whereas the GA optimized grating structure to minimize 
the product of L (length) and q max (maximum magnitude of in-
dex modulation profile), significantly enhancing spectral notch 
depth and reducing interline loss by 100× for 4th-order super-
Gaussian profiles. On the other hand, Adibnia et al. 111 applied 
DL for the ID of chirped apodized FBGs. The DNN model ac-
curately mapped FBG design parameters to reflective spectra 
across various chirp and apodization profiles, achieving low 
training loss. The inverse model addressed the many-to-one 
mapping challenge, enabling precise prediction of FBG struc-
tures from target spectra, eliminating reliance on iterative or em-
pirical methods. The major finding of their work is presented in 
Fig. 16(a). Subsequently, the same group developed a hybrid DL 
approach that combines a CNN and a multilayer perceptron 
(MLP) to inversely design dual-band FBG-based optical filters 
with high accuracy. 112 The CNN extracts apodization profiles 
from target spectra, whereas the MLP maps grating parameters 
to spectral responses. Their model achieved low predictive 
losses (∼1.0 × 10 −2 ) for both forward and inverse tasks with 
<5% loss variation over 10 independent training runs. Inversely 
designed single-chirped and apodized FBG demonstrated a 
dual-transmission band with a full width at half-maxima 
(FWHM) of 0.747 nm.
Although Xu et al. 108 enhanced the standard tandem network 
by refining the loss function, Liu et al. 109 proposed a fundamen-
tally different approach, leveraging probability distributions 
and CE loss to address the one-to-many problem, showing 
robustness even with limited data. Du et al. 107 introduced a
predictor network combined with PSO as an efficient alternative, 
particularly effective when target specifications involve dominant 
spectral features rather than exact spectra. Hejazi et al. 18 comple-
mented these trends using GDA over a differentiable NN model 
to enable precise ID. Similarly, Yu et al. 110 and Adibnia et al. 111,112 
demonstrated the ability of ML methods to handle the complex 
design space of advanced FBGs. Although Yu 110 offered a hybrid 
ANN-GA strategy for AFBG optimization, Adibnia 111 adopted a 
pure DL framework for both forward prediction and ID, and later 
demonstrated combining DL with CNN to streamline the ID 
process. 112 Most recently, Yeung et al. 113 introduced a multiclass 
DL framework that combines supervised learning with PPO-
based RL for the inverse design of SOI metagratings. In this 
hybrid strategy, a CNN generates an initial design that is sub-
sequently refined by an RL agent, extending the capabilities 
of each method while reducing training data dependence and im-
proving overall generalizability. Together, these works highlight 
the versatility of ML-driven ID across diverse grating-based sys-
tems, from couplers to filters to mode converters.
6.3 Mode Selective Couplers
MSCs have applications in MDM and have also benefited from 
ML-based inverse design. Zhang et al. 23 employed a DNN for 
the ID of UWB fiber-optic MSCs composed of a bridge fiber 
and an FMF. The DNN maps the effective RIs (n eff ) of target 
higher-order modes (HOMs) in the FMF to bridge fiber param-
eters (core diameter D core , doping mole fraction X) to enable 
rapid phase-matching optimization between the LP 01 mode of 
the bridge fiber and any HOM of the FMF. This approach 
significantly reduced optimization time (45 s versus 4 h with 
a GA) while achieving UWB phase matching for 4-LP MSCs 
(mode conversion efficiency ½MCE  > 93%, mode extinction 
ratio ½MER  > 17 dB over S+C+L band) and high-purity orbital

Furthermore, Liang et al. 115 developed a physics-guided two-
stage NN for designing photonic-plasmonic couplers for super-

These studies demonstrated that ML-based ID significantly 
accelerates the development of complex optical couplers by 
replacing computationally intensive methods such as FDTD 
simulations coupled with GAs 23 or manual analysis. 114 Beyond 
speed, ML enables direct optimization of target performance 
metrics such as specific coupling behavior, 114 UWB phase 
matching, 23 and high-efficiency plasmonic mode excitation. 115 
Moreover, the integration of physics-guided models enhances 
interpretability and accuracy while addressing challenges such 
as dimensional mismatches. 115
6.4 Failure Modes and Open Problems—Optical 
Components
• Efficiency-compactness trade-off: Many inverse-designed 
devices prioritize smaller footprints over efficiency. Using 
multi-objective optimization with fabrication-aware loss terms 
can balance insertion loss, size, and manufacturability.
• Computational burden: Each new geometry still demands 
large datasets. Transfer learning or attention-based architectures 
could reduce training time and support scalable multi-input and 
multi-output designs.
• Physically infeasible outputs: Networks may produce 
parameters that are not physically achievable. Using architec-
tures with constraints or custom activation layers can help guar-
antee physical feasibility.
• Broader functionality and validation: Future research 
should expand inverse-design methods to more complex MIMO 
photonic circuits and conduct experimental performance verifi-
cation to confirm their reliability in practical applications.
7 Inverse Design of Optical Fibers
ML has emerged as a transformative paradigm in the inverse 
design of optical fibers, offering powerful tools to navigate 
complex design spaces and discover structures with desired 
functionalities. This section summarizes recent advancements 
where ML-driven inverse design has been applied to various 
types of optical fibers, including FMFs, 116–118 photonic crystal 
fibers (PCFs), 119–122 and other specialty fibers for diverse 
applications. 123–125 Table 4 comprehensively summarizes recent
progress in ML-assisted inverse design of optical fibers, detail-
ing the underlying methodologies, design objectives, and per-
formance outcomes across various fiber types.
7.1 Few-Mode Fibers (FMFs)
FMFs are critical for expanding the capacity of optical commu-
nication systems through MDM. The design of FMFs, espe-
cially those ensuring weak coupling between modes to 
minimize or eliminate multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) 
digital signal processing, presents a significant challenge. 
Recent works 116,117 pioneered the use of NNs for the inverse de-
sign of weakly coupled FMFs, demonstrating high accuracy and 
efficiency, offering a reusable design solution. For instance, He 
et al. 116 successfully designed four-ring and six-ring step-index 
FMFs supporting 4, 6, 10, and 20 modes. The approach focused 
on optimizing the minimum effective RI difference (Δn eff;min ) 
between adjacent mode groups, achieving values > 10 −3 for 
20-mode FMFs. Similarly, Guo et al. 117 employed both ANNs 
and random forest (RF) algorithms for the ID of vector modes-
based weak-coupling FMFs, specifically targeting a high-
contrast high-RI fiber structure supporting six vector modes. 
Addressing the design of more complex FMF structures, 
Napiorkowski et al. 118 utilized generative inverse design networks 
(GIDNs) to optimize spatial mode separation in nanostructured 
FMFs. Their novel approach, tailored to FMF properties, com-
bined a ring-assisted design with noncylindrical symmetry using 
thousands of subwavelength glass rods. This method achieved a 
significant minimum effective RI difference ðMinjΔn eff jÞ > 
1.2 × 10 −3 for 10 spatial modes while notably reducing the re-
quired training dataset size by a factor of 6 compared with tradi-
tional DNN optimization. Figure 17 shows the results of this work. 
These studies collectively demonstrated the power of ML in de-
signing FMFs with specific coupling characteristics. A common 
gap addressed by these papers is the inefficiency of traditional 
parameter sweeping for multiparameter FMF optimization.
7.2 Photonic Crystal Fibers (PCFs)
In the realm of PCF dispersion compensation, Refs. 119 and 120 
explored the potential of DNNs, offering high accuracy and ef-
ficient inverse designs. Ma et al. 119 designed a Ge-doped dual-
core dispersion compensation PCF using an ANN combined 
with a DE algorithm. The designed fiber showcased impressive 
broadband dispersion compensation capabilities across S-C-L-
U and E-S-C-L-U bands, with Kappa values closely matching 
standard single-mode fibers like SMF28. Next, Gan et al. 120 em-
ployed a DLNN for the dispersion-oriented inverse design of 
PCF optimized for four-wave mixing (FWM) applications. A 
key innovation was rapidly generating a comprehensive dataset 
via an empirical PCF dispersion formula, outperforming FEM 
simulations in speed. The DNN predicted PCF parameters with 
over 95% accuracy, validated experimentally by designing a 
PCF for 1064 to 770 nm wavelength conversion for potential 
applications in biomedical imaging.
On the other hand, targeting optical sensing applications, 
Tang et al. 121 introduced a bidirectional design approach com-
bining DNNs and an augmented gray wolf optimizer (AGWO) 
for SPP-based PCF magnetic field sensors. A forward prediction 
network (FPN) was proposed to accurately predict optical re-
sponses (e.g., confinement loss, transmission, and effective 
RI) from sensor geometries, achieving an R 2 value of 0.9942

7.3 Other Specialty Fibers
Beyond FMFs and PCFs, ML-based inverse design has been 
successfully applied to a variety of specialty optical fibers, 123–125
each tailored for specific applications. For instance, Guo et al. 123 
utilized ANNs for the inverse design of M-type fibers intended 
to generate flat-top (FT) beams, which are crucial for high-
power fiber laser applications such as welding and cutting. 
Their ANN-based method successfully determined the structural 
parameters for optimal FT beam performance, characterized by 
high flatness, power confinement factor, and effective area. For 
OAM transmission, Qin et al. 124 introduced a hybrid method 
combining NNs with the PSO algorithm and multi-objective 
PSO (MOPSO). The hybrid PSO-NN and MOPSO-NN meth-
ods efficiently searched for optimal OAM fiber structures, 
achieving high Δn eff > 110 −4 and low dispersion. Meng 
et al. 125 aimed to minimize confinement loss (CL) and addressed 
the non-uniqueness challenge in inverse design of hollow-core 
anti-resonant fibers (HC-ARFs) using a tandem NN architec-
ture, as shown in Fig. 18. The tandem NN model demonstrated 
effectiveness with an MSE of 0.0007 and a coefficient of deter-
mination R 2 up to 0.9154 for predicting CL, and R2
CL up to
0.9881 for predicting specific datasets, accelerating HC-ARF 
design. Table 4 highlights the core details of the ML architec-
tures discussed in this section. These studies underscore the ver-
satility of ML in designing diverse fiber types. Although some 
rely on standalone NNs, 123 others adopt hybrid NN-optimizer 
strategies 124 or specialized NN architectures such as tandem

7.4 Failure Modes and Open Problems—Optical Fibers
• Limited spectral scope: Most models focus on narrow 
bands. Expanding datasets and loss functions for broadband 
dispersion management is crucial.
• Neglect of higher-order modes: Current methods primarily 
address fundamental modes. Incorporating higher-order and 
multimode interactions can enhance accuracy in many appli-
cations.
• Fabrication-tolerance 
modeling: 
Structural 
deviations 
during drawing are rarely modeled. Robust optimization and 
adaptive prediction of tolerance effects are required for reliable
production. 
• Incomplete physical parameter integration: Effects of 
polarization, curvature, temperature, and micro-deformations 
are often ignored; their inclusion would strengthen real-world 
performance.
8 Inverse Design of Optical Detectors
Inverse design integrated with ML approaches has yet to see 
significant application in the development of optical detectors, 
representing a notable gap in current research. Meanwhile, 
metamaterials have attracted considerable interest in recent 
years due to their potential to enhance optical absorption and 
tailor spectral responses. These properties make them strong 
candidates for integration with photodiodes, thus achieving a 
tailored targeted optical detector performance for applications 
in optical communication and sensing. 126–128 Consequently, 
the following section reviews recent advancements 129–134 in
the inverse design of MMAs, with a focus on approaches as-
sisted by ML techniques, and comprehensively summarized in 
Table 5.
In 2020, Hou et al. 129 introduced a target-driven DL method 
using the desired frequency domain absorption spectrum to map 
MMA geometry/material parameters. The MMA (geometrical, 
structural, and material parameters) was designed using a fea-
ture transformer neural network (FTNN), a generator neural 
network (GNN), and a nine-hidden-layer PNN. The FTNN con-
verted performance indices (e.g., band limits and slope) to a tar-
get spectrum for the GNN. Trained with >22; 000 samples, the 
model achieved 93.5% test accuracy, enabling on-demand MMA 
design based on specific performance indices. Subsequently, 
Han et al. 130 designed an MMA with polygonal meta-atoms 
for tunable single-layer dual-band absorption. The MMA 
Al 2 O 3 ∕Ag structure with eight tunable vertices was optimized 
using a spectra predicting network (SPN) and an improved de-
sign predicting network (DPN). The SPN predicted spectra with 
MSE 0.0008, whereas the DPN predicted structures with MSE 
between 0.0035 and 0.0026. The predicted spectrum showcased 
a single peak with >96% absorptivity for incident light angles 
on the MMA ranging from −50 to 50 deg.
On the other hand, Wang et al. 131 employed a residual fully 
connected neural network (RFC-NN) for inverse designing 
a five-layer SiO 2 -Ti-SiO 2 -Ti-SiO 2 MMA to achieve an ultra-
broadband absorption covering 400 to 2600 nm. The RFC-NN 
surpassed FCNN and tandem NN in accuracy and generaliza-
tion, and the model engaged the Pearson correlation coefficient
