The failure modes and open problems of the optical detectors 
are listed as follows.
• Lack of experimental validation: Most inverse-designed 
detectors are still mainly simulated. Real-world fabrication 
and testing are necessary to confirm expected responsivity 
and how well they tolerate imperfections.
• Limited structural exploration: Conventional geometries 
dominate current designs. Exploring irregular, asymmetric, or 
fractal structures with advanced inverse algorithms might lead 
to increased absorption and sensitivity.
• Fabrication challenges: Complex geometries require more 
precise manufacturing control. Advances in high-precision 
lithography, nanoimprinting, or additive nanofabrication are 
necessary for practical use.
• Spectral selectivity control: Achieving tunable, applica-
tion-specific absorption spectra continues to be a challenge. 
Future research should focus on inverse frameworks that can 
design exact spectral responses for sensing and photovoltaic 
applications.

the key challenges deduced from this review, along with some 
potential avenues for future research.

9.1 Present Status of ML-Driven ID Approaches
A summary of the current status of various ML architectures, 
categorized in five ML paradigms, is illustrated in Fig. 20. 
This paradigm-based classification framework allows us to 
analyze how ML techniques address the inherent challenges of 
inverse design in a more structured way. It illustrates the ML 
methods used for each optical device class and, for each 
method-device pair, shows how often the surveyed articles in-
dicate low, medium, or high data demand (defined respectively 
as <10k, 10k to 100k, and >100k training samples), computing 
cost, and design uniqueness. MLPs are employed across nearly 
all device types and ML paradigms, with entries spanning the 
entire spectrum of data and compute levels, including both 
unique and nonunique designs. This suggests that resource re-
quirements and novelty are mainly influenced by the complexity 
of the ID task, rather than the use of MLP itself. More complex
hybrid models combining MLPs with CNNs, ResNets, or se-
quence models are common for amplifiers, gratings/metagratings, 
some fibers, and metamaterial absorbers, and occasionally in 
laser studies. Many of these are characterized by medium- to 
high-data-demand and compute-cost ranges, consistent with 
larger, more detailed design spaces. Generative models (GAN, 
VAE, CVAE) are limited to a few device types, such as semi-
conductor lasers, power/beam splitters, and some gratings and 
couplers. For example, one GAN-based laser study is classified 
as both data- and compute-intensive and highly unique, whereas 
a CVAE-ResNet power splitter is low-cost and nonunique. VAE-
assisted designs for gratings/couplers fall between these ex-
tremes, showing that generative architectures can support both 
conventional and innovative designs depending on the applica-
tion. Device type also influences the results: several metamate-
rial-absorber detector entries exhibit high uniqueness with low-
to-moderate data and compute demands, whereas most laser and

9.2 Key Challenges and Future Research Directions
Inverse design, enabled by ML approaches, offers a powerful 
and flexible framework for designing photonic devices and 
components by uncovering nonintuitive, performance-opti-
mized configurations. The increasing use of ML-based ID is evi-
dent in the development of optical sources, amplifiers, passive 
components, detectors, and fibers. However, most recent studies 
focused on optimizing individual elements without addressing 
specific application scenarios. Notably absent are system-level 
ID strategies that consider the interdependencies among multi-
ple components crucial for optical communication and sensing, 
where coordinated optimization across modules, such as sources, 
filters, amplifiers, waveguides, and detectors, is essential. 
Among individual devices, optical detectors remain a par-
ticularly underexplored area, with few ID implementations 
and even fewer targeting application-specific requirements rel-
evant to communication and sensing. To date, ML-based ID of 
detectors for telecommunication applications is nonexistent. 
Moreover, the ML-based ID approach has not been applied to 
optical modulators, although there are some examples of ID of 
modulators using optimization algorithms. 137,138 This is possibly 
due to the scarcity of training data as the multiphysics simula-
tions required for training such a system are computationally 
expensive. There is also a pressing need to expand the ID efforts 
for optical components in fiber platforms, which remain under-
represented despite their widespread use in communication and 
sensing infrastructure. Overall, the lack of an integrated, appli-
cation-driven system design represents a critical gap. 
Standalone devices and components face several challenges. 
A major obstacle is the scarcity of experimentally obtained 
datasets for training models, coupled with limited experimental 
validation of inverse-designed devices. Most designs rely 
solely on simulations, raising concerns about their real-world 
viability. 119,124,125,129,133 Fabrication-related structural uncertainties 
are also not given due importance in most of the inverse design 
solutions proposed in the literature. Some performance deviation 
between simulated and fabricated devices is always expected 
due to issues related to lithographic line-edge roughness, side-
wall angle variation, and etching anisotropy. These limitations 
are especially present in optical sources and fibers, 73 where 
even small structural inconsistencies can significantly change 
dispersion or confinement properties. For optical fibers, emerg-
ing ML-based real-time correction techniques for fabrication 
errors have been proposed; however, these are still in an early 
stage of development. 116,119 In addition, the need for retraining 
models for each new task limits scalability. 76,80 Besides these, 
other important challenges include the need for improved gen-
eralization to complex geometries and nonlinear media, 93,134 as 
well as inadequate modeling of fabrication uncertainties. 73,76 
To overcome the fabrication-related discrepancies, the con-
cept of fabrication-aware inverse design (FAID) has recently
gained importance and has inspired some techniques in the lit-
erature. FAID takes into consideration the fabrication process 
limitations while solving the inverse design optimization prob-
lem. Unlike conventional inverse design approaches that assume 
ideal geometries, FAID introduces manufacturing constraints in 
the optimization process, such as roughness, corner rounding, 
and sidewall slope. This type of optimization ensures that the 
inverse design solution performance closely matches the target 
specifications, thereby ensuring the design quality. Recent works 
have demonstrated both data-driven and physics-driven FAID 
solutions, including neural fabrication models and lithography-
aware optimization techniques. 139–142 FAID substantially enhances 
the robustness, reproducibility, and manufacturability of inverse-
designed photonic devices, thus accelerating the transition from 
simulation-based optimization to real-world production. 
Interest in FAID has introduced several research directions. 
For example, experimental validation must be prioritized across 
all device categories to ensure real-world applicability. For 
optical amplifiers, extending experimental validation to cover 
the C+L bands using tunable Raman pumps, coupled with 
PINNs, could enhance model reliability and reduce data 
dependency. 43,44,83,93 In addition to that, FAID-based solutions 
for optical sources should consider more output parameters in 
the design process as optimization objectives. Similarly, RL 
approaches could offer improved performance in high-dimen-
sional spaces. 76,80
In this context, transformer-based architectures hold promise 
for modeling the complex relationship between physical struc-
ture and the response of photonic devices, especially when 
inverse mappings involve long-range dependencies or inputs re-
lated to the spatial structure. Moreover, large language models 
can accelerate the overall design cycle by realizing several im-
portant tasks, such as generating simulation code, proposing de-
sign constraints, and guiding surrogate model construction. This 
can be achieved when the large language models are fine-tuned 
on domain-specific data; however, this solution requires signifi-
cant amounts of data. Data scarcity may also motivate solutions 
related to small language models that could provide similar per-
formance but with less data requirements.
Inverse design strategies should increasingly target system-
level optimization, moving beyond isolated devices to simulta-
neously optimize interdependent photonic elements for ISAC 
tasks. Bridging this gap requires a conceptual shift that aligns 
the intrinsic strengths of inverse design with the multifaceted 
performance requirements of real-world optical communication 
and sensing platforms. Progress in this direction will pave the 
way for intelligent, application-specific photonic systems.
10 Conclusion
In summary, through a comprehensive discussion on inverse 
design of optical devices and components, as well as critically 
evaluating across the five ML paradigms, this review under-
scores the transformative potential of machine learning models 
in photonics. In particular, the adoption of various ML models’ 
capabilities to handle high-dimensional, nonlinear relationships 
inherent in photonic devices. These models facilitated rapid de-
sign space exploration and significantly reduced computational 
burdens, enabling efficient optimization of optical structures 
that would be challenging to realize using traditional methods. 
Although substantial advances have been achieved in design-
ing active devices such as semiconductor lasers, fiber lasers, and 
optical amplifiers using ML-driven ID approaches, emerging

embedding governing physical laws into the design process, 
offering improved generalization with limited data availability. 
As inverse design continues to evolve, its role in enabling 
compact, multifunctional, and high-performance photonic sys-
tems will be central to the development of future optical com-
munication, sensing, and integrated photonic networks. This 
convergence of data-driven intelligence and physical modeling 
sets the stage for more adaptive and scalable photonic technol-
ogies, tailored to meet the growing demands of next-generation 
optical infrastructures.
